Content: TCS food must cool from 135°F to 70°F within 2 hours then from 70°F to 41°F within 4 more hours for a total cooling window of 6 hours.
KeyPoints: 135°F to 70°F within 2 hours|70°F to 41°F within 4 more hours|Total window: 6 hours

Content: You missed cooling questions. The rule is two stages — 135 to 70 in 2 hours then 70 to 41 in 4 more hours. Total window is 6 hours.
Cooling has two steps. Think of it as a race with two legs. First leg — get from 135 down to 70 in 2 hours. Second leg — finish to 41 in 4 more hours. Miss either leg and you start over.
Approved rapid cooling methods include ice baths ice paddles shallow pans and blast chillers. The goal is to maximize surface area and move heat out of the food as fast as possible.
KeyPoints: Ice baths|Ice paddles|Shallow pans|Blast chillers|Goal: maximize surface area

Content: When cooling questions come up think surface area. Shallow pans ice baths and blast chillers all work because they expose more food to cold. Deep pots on a counter do not.
Here is the secret to cooling — the more surface area you expose the faster it cools. A shallow pan beats a deep pot every time. An ice bath beats a walk-in cooler every time. Think thin and cold.
If food does not reach 70°F within the first 2 hours it must be reheated to 165°F and the cooling process restarted. You cannot simply continue cooling food that has failed the first stage.
KeyPoints: Food fails stage 1: reheat to 165°F|Restart cooling process|Cannot continue cooling failed food

Content: If the first cooling stage fails — reheat to 165 and start over. Continuing to cool food that missed the 2-hour window is never correct on the exam.

If your food did not cool fast enough in the first two hours do not just keep going. You have to reheat it all the way to 165 and start the cooling process from scratch. Think of it as resetting the clock.
Ready-to-eat TCS food held at 41°F or below must be date marked and discarded after 7 days. The day of preparation counts as day one.
KeyPoints: Ready-to-eat TCS food held at 41°F or below|Must be date marked|Discard after 7 days|Day of prep = Day 1

Content: Date marking questions always involve the number 7. The prep day is day one. Count 7 days including the prep day and that is your discard date.
Every ready-to-eat food in your cooler needs a label that says when to throw it out. Count 7 days starting with the day you made it. Day one is today. Day seven is the last day you can serve it.
Any ready-to-eat TCS food held longer than 24 hours must be date marked. This includes cooked proteins sliced deli meats opened dairy products and prepared salads.
KeyPoints: Any ready-to-eat TCS food held longer than 24 hours|Must be date marked|Cooked proteins

Content: The exam tests whether you know which foods need date marks. If it is a ready-to-eat TCS food held more than 24 hours it needs a date mark. No exceptions.
If you cooked it sliced it or opened it and it needs to stay cold — it needs a date on it. Cooked chicken sliced turkey opened sour cream prepared pasta salad — all of them. When in doubt label it.
TCS food held hot for service must be maintained at 135°F or above at all times. Food that drops below 135°F has entered the temperature danger zone.
KeyPoints: Hot TCS food must stay at 135°F or above|Below 135°F = danger zone

Content: Hot holding is 135 and above. Any answer that suggests a lower temperature for hot holding is wrong. If food drops below 135 it must be reheated to 165 before returning to service.
Your steam table your heat lamp your soup warmer — everything hot needs to stay at 135 or above. Think of 135 as the floor. Anything below that floor and bacteria start to grow.
If hot-held food drops below 135°F it must be reheated to 165°F for 15 seconds before being returned to the holding unit. Stirring or adding hot liquid is not an approved corrective action.
KeyPoints: Food drops below 135°F: reheat to 165°F for 15 seconds|Return to holding unit|Stirring or adding hot liquid is NOT approved

Content: When hot food drops below 135 the only correct answer is reheat to 165. The exam will offer stirring or adding hot liquid as distractors. Neither is correct.
If your soup drops to 120 on the steam table you cannot just stir it or add hot broth. You have to take it off pull it to the stove and get it all the way back to 165. Then it can go back out.
TCS food held cold for service must be maintained at 41°F or below at all times. Food that rises above 41°F has entered the temperature danger zone.
KeyPoints: Cold TCS food must stay at 41°F or below|Above 41°F = danger zone|4-hour cumulative rule: discard if exceeded

Content: Cold holding is 41 and below. The 4-hour rule applies — if TCS food has been above 41 for a cumulative total of 4 hours it must be discarded.

Your salad bar your deli case your prep table with cold items — everything cold needs to stay at 41 or below. Think of 41 as the ceiling. Anything above that ceiling and you are in danger zone territory.
Poultry including chicken turkey and duck must reach a minimum internal temperature of 165°F for 15 seconds. This applies to whole birds parts ground poultry and stuffed poultry.
KeyPoints: Poultry: 165°F for 15 seconds|Applies to whole birds

Content: 165 for 15 seconds is poultry. This is the highest standard cooking temperature. Stuffed items also require 165 because the stuffing contacts raw poultry juices.

Chicken turkey duck — they all need to hit 165. That is the highest cooking temperature you will deal with. And if you stuff the bird the stuffing has to hit 165 too because it soaked up the raw juices.
Ground meat including beef pork and commercially raised game must reach 155°F for 15 seconds. Grinding spreads surface pathogens throughout the product requiring a higher temperature than whole cuts.
KeyPoints: Ground meat: 155°F for 15 seconds|Beef

Content: 155 for 15 seconds is ground meat. The exam loves to offer 165 as a distractor for ground beef. It is wrong. Ground beef is 155. Only poultry and stuffed items require 165.
When meat gets ground up the bacteria that lived on the outside get mixed all the way through. That is why your burger needs to hit 155 — hotter than a steak but not as hot as chicken.

ConceptTitle: Seafood and Whole Cut Cooking Temperature
Content: Seafood and whole muscle cuts of beef pork and lamb must reach 145°F for 15 seconds. Whole cuts keep their pathogens on the surface where heat reaches them quickly.
KeyPoints: Seafood and whole cuts: 145°F for 15 seconds|Steaks

ConceptTitle: Seafood and Whole Cut Cooking Temperature
Content: 145 for 15 seconds covers seafood and whole cuts — steaks chops and roasts. Remember the sequence — Poultry 165 Ground 155 Whole cuts and seafood 145.

ConceptTitle: Seafood and Whole Cut Cooking Temperature
Content: A steak or a fish fillet only has bacteria on the outside. Heat gets there fast. That is why 145 is enough. Think of it as the lowest rung on the cooking temperature ladder.
Fruits and vegetables cooked for hot holding must reach 135°F. This is the lowest required cooking temperature and applies to items like cooked rice beans and roasted vegetables.
KeyPoints: Fruits

Content: 135 is the cooking temperature for fruits vegetables and grains held hot. It is also the minimum hot holding temperature — same number two uses.

Cooked rice cooked beans roasted vegetables — they just need to hit 135. That is the same temperature they need to stay at in the steam table. One number covers both cooking and holding for these items.
All TCS food reheated for hot holding must reach 165°F for 15 seconds regardless of its original cooking temperature. This destroys any pathogens that grew during storage or cooling.
KeyPoints: All TCS food reheated for hot holding: 165°F for 15 seconds|Regardless of original cooking temperature

Content: Reheating temperature is always 165 — no exceptions. The exam will try to confuse you with the original cooking temperature. It does not matter. Reheat always means 165.

It does not matter if you originally cooked it to 145. When you reheat it you go all the way to 165. Every time. No shortcuts. The food sat in a cooler where bacteria may have grown and 165 is what kills them.
Food reheated in a microwave must reach 165°F throughout and then rest covered for 2 minutes to allow heat to distribute evenly before the temperature is verified.
KeyPoints: Microwave reheat: 165°F throughout|Rest covered for 2 minutes|Allows heat to distribute evenly

Content: Microwave reheating has two requirements — 165°F and a 2-minute covered rest. Both must happen. The exam will test whether you know about the rest period.
Microwaves heat unevenly — one spot might be 180 while another is 140. That is why after you microwave food to 165 you cover it and let it sit for 2 minutes. The heat evens out during that rest.

ConceptTitle: Time as a Public Health Control
Content: When using time as a control for cold food the maximum hold time is 6 hours and the food must start at 41°F or below and never exceed 70°F. A discard time must be marked on the food.
KeyPoints: Cold food max hold time: 6 hours|Must start at 41°F or below|Must never exceed 70°F|Mark discard time on food

ConceptTitle: Time as a Public Health Control
Content: Time as control questions involve two numbers — 6 hours maximum and 70°F maximum temperature. If either limit is exceeded the food must be discarded immediately.

ConceptTitle: Time as a Public Health Control
Content: Sometimes you cannot keep food cold — like at an outdoor event. The rules say you get 6 hours maximum and the food can never go above 70 degrees. Write the discard time on the label before you put it out.
TCS foods are those that require time and temperature control for safety. They include meat poultry seafood dairy eggs cooked starches cut melons cut leafy greens and cut tomatoes.
KeyPoints: Meat

Content: TCS food identification is a common exam topic. Cut produce — melons tomatoes leafy greens — becomes TCS when cut. Whole uncut produce and dry shelf-stable items are not TCS.

TCS foods are the ones that can make people sick if you leave them out too long. Meat fish chicken eggs dairy cooked rice cooked pasta — and cut fruits and vegetables. If it is moist and protein-rich it is probably TCS.

Food in a refrigerator must be stored from top to bottom in order of minimum required cooking temperature. Ready-to-eat food on top then seafood then whole cuts then ground meat then poultry on the bottom.
KeyPoints: Top to bottom: Ready-to-eat → Seafood → Whole cuts → Ground meat → Poultry|Order by minimum cooking temperature lowest to highest

Content: Storage order is one of the most tested topics. Top to bottom — ready-to-eat seafood whole cuts ground meat poultry. The mnemonic Really Safe Cooks Go Places helps remember the order.

Think of the cooler like a building. The safest food lives on the top floor. The riskiest food lives in the basement. Ready-to-eat on top. Raw chicken on the bottom. Everything else in between by cooking temperature.
The nine major food allergens recognized by the FDA are milk eggs fish shellfish tree nuts peanuts wheat soybeans and sesame. Even trace amounts can trigger a life-threatening reaction.
KeyPoints: Milk

Content: Nine allergens — milk eggs fish shellfish tree nuts peanuts wheat soybeans sesame. The exam tests whether you can identify them and understand that cross-contact with even a trace amount is dangerous.

Nine foods can kill someone with an allergy. Milk eggs fish shellfish tree nuts peanuts wheat soybeans and sesame. You do not have to use a lot of them. Even a tiny amount left on a pan or a spoon is enough to cause a serious reaction.

Cross-contact occurs when an allergen is transferred from one food to another through shared equipment surfaces or utensils. Unlike cross-contamination with pathogens allergen cross-contact cannot be eliminated by cooking.
KeyPoints: Allergen transferred via shared equipment

Content: Cross-contact is allergen-specific. The key difference from cross-contamination — cooking does not destroy allergens. A peanut-contaminated pan that is heated to 165 is still a peanut-contaminated pan.

Bacteria die when you cook food hot enough. Allergens do not. If peanut residue gets on a pan and you cook something else in that pan the allergen is still there. The only fix is to wash rinse and sanitize completely.
Allergen-free meals must be prepared using dedicated equipment and surfaces that have been cleaned and sanitized. They should be prepared first before any allergen-containing items are handled.
KeyPoints: Use dedicated equipment and surfaces|Clean and sanitize before use|Prepare allergen-free meals first

Content: For allergen questions the answer always involves separation — separate equipment separate surfaces prepare first. Any answer that suggests shared equipment with extra sanitizing is wrong.

When a customer tells you they have an allergy treat it like a fire drill. Stop what you are doing. Get clean equipment. Wash your hands. Make their food first before you touch anything with the allergen in it.
Cross-contamination is prevented through proper storage order dedicated equipment handwashing glove changes and sanitizing surfaces between tasks. The most common source is hands and equipment.
KeyPoints: Proper storage order|Dedicated equipment|Handwashing and glove changes|Sanitize surfaces between tasks

Content: Cross-contamination questions test whether you know the correct preventive action. Dedicated equipment handwashing and proper storage order are always correct. Shared equipment with rinse-only cleaning is always wrong.

Cross-contamination means moving bacteria from one place to another — usually from raw meat to food that will not be cooked again. Your hands your gloves your cutting board your tongs — all of them can be the vehicle.

Single-use gloves must be changed after switching tasks after handling raw proteins after touching non-food-contact surfaces and after extended wear. Gloves do not replace handwashing — hands must be washed before gloving.
KeyPoints: Change after switching tasks|Change after handling raw proteins|Change after touching non-food surfaces|Gloves do NOT replace handwashing

Content: Glove questions test two things — when to change them and whether they replace handwashing. They never replace handwashing. Change them after every task switch after raw protein contact and after touching equipment.

Gloves are not a shield that protects everything. They get contaminated just like hands do. Change them when you switch tasks. And always wash your hands before you put on a new pair — dirty hands make dirty gloves.
The four approved thawing methods are refrigeration running water at 70°F or below microwave if cooked immediately and cooking from frozen. Room temperature thawing is never acceptable.
KeyPoints: Refrigerator at 41°F or below|Running water at 70°F or below|Microwave — cook immediately after|Cooking from frozen|Counter thawing: never acceptable

Content: Thawing questions come down to one principle — keep the food out of the danger zone. Refrigeration running cold water microwave-then-cook and cooking from frozen all do that. Counter thawing does not.

Never thaw food on the counter. By the time the inside is thawed the outside has been sitting in the danger zone for hours. Put it in the fridge overnight run cold water over it or cook it from frozen.
Chemicals must be stored in their original labeled containers in a designated area completely separate from all food food-contact equipment and single-use items to prevent contamination.
KeyPoints: Store in original labeled containers|Designated area completely separate from food|Away from food-contact equipment and single-use items

Content: Chemical storage questions — the answer is always a completely separate area away from food and equipment. The exam will offer top shelf storage as a distractor. Top shelf is wrong.

Chemicals and food should never be in the same space. Not the same shelf. Not the same room if possible. One spill one leak one mislabeled bottle and someone gets sick from something that had nothing to do with bacteria.
The correct handwashing sequence is wet hands, apply soap, scrub for at least 10 to 15 seconds, rinse thoroughly, and dry with a single-use towel or air dryer. Water must be at a minimum of 100°F.
KeyPoints: Wet hands → apply soap → scrub 10–15 seconds → rinse → dry with single-use towel|Water minimum 100°F

Content: Handwashing procedure questions test the sequence and the time. Wet soap scrub rinse dry. Scrubbing is 10 to 15 seconds. Water minimum 100°F. Hand sanitizer is never a substitute.

Washing your hands the right way takes about 20 seconds total. Wet them. Soap up. Scrub everything — backs of hands between fingers under nails — for at least 10 seconds. Rinse. Dry with a paper towel. That is it.
Food handlers must wash their hands after using the restroom handling raw meat or poultry touching the face or hair handling garbage handling money sneezing coughing and before putting on gloves.
KeyPoints: After restroom|After handling raw meat or poultry|After touching face or hair|After handling garbage or money|Before putting on gloves

Content: Handwashing trigger questions — after restroom after raw meat after touching face after garbage after money before gloving. Any of these situations on the exam means wash hands. No shortcuts.

Think of your hands as a transfer vehicle. Everything you touch puts something on your hands. Everything you touch after that picks it up. The restroom raw chicken the trash the phone — all of them require a handwash before you go back to food.
The five reportable illnesses are Norovirus Hepatitis A Salmonella Typhi Shigella and E. coli O157:H7. These require both exclusion from the operation and notification of the regulatory authority.
KeyPoints: Norovirus|Hepatitis A|Salmonella Typhi|Shigella|E. coli O157:H7|All require regulatory notification

Content: Five reportable illnesses — Norovirus Hepatitis A Salmonella Typhi Shigella E. coli O157:H7. The acronym SHEEN helps — Salmonella Hepatitis E. coli E for excluded and Norovirus. All five require regulatory notification.

Five illnesses are so serious that you have to tell the health department — not just send the employee home. Norovirus Hepatitis A Salmonella Typhi Shigella and E. coli O157:H7. These are the big five. Know them cold.

Exclusion means the food handler must leave the operation entirely. Restriction means they may stay but cannot work with food or food-contact surfaces. Vomiting diarrhea and jaundice always require exclusion.
KeyPoints: Exclusion: leave operation entirely|Restriction: stay but no food or food-contact surfaces|Vomiting

Content: Exclusion vs restriction is a key exam distinction. Vomiting diarrhea jaundice — always exclusion. Sore throat with fever may allow restriction in some operations. When in doubt the exam favors exclusion.

There is a difference between sending someone home and restricting them to non-food work. Vomiting and diarrhea — they go home completely. A sore throat with no fever — they might be able to work the register or wash dishes. Use your judgment and always protect the food.

Food handlers must wear effective hair restraints to keep hair out of food. The only jewelry permitted is a plain band ring. Watches bracelets and rings with stones are prohibited because they harbor pathogens.
KeyPoints: Effective hair restraint required|Only plain band ring permitted|No watches

Content: Jewelry questions — plain band ring only. Everything else is prohibited. Hair restraint questions — any effective restraint is acceptable not just hair nets.

Keep your hair covered and your jewelry simple. A plain wedding band is fine. A watch a bracelet a ring with a stone — those all have tiny spaces where food and bacteria hide. Leave them in your locker.

An infected wound on the hands or wrists must be covered with an impermeable bandage and then protected with a single-use glove before handling food. Wounds on other body parts must be covered with a bandage.
KeyPoints: Infected wound on hands or wrists: bandage + single-use glove|Bandage alone is not sufficient|Glove goes over bandage

Content: Wound questions — bandage plus glove for hands and wrists. Bandage alone is not sufficient for a hand wound. The glove goes over the bandage.

A cut on your hand is a direct path for bacteria to get into food. Cover it with a bandage then put a glove over the bandage. The bandage alone is not enough — gloves can slip and bandages can fall off.
Cold TCS food must be received at 41°F or below. Shell eggs and shucked shellfish may be received at 45°F. Live shellfish must be received at an air temperature of 45°F or below.
KeyPoints: Cold TCS food: 41°F or below|Shell eggs and shucked shellfish: 45°F|Live shellfish: 45°F air temperature

Content: Receiving temperature questions — 41°F for most cold TCS foods 45°F for shell eggs shucked shellfish and live shellfish air temperature. These exceptions appear frequently on the exam.

When a delivery arrives the first thing you do is check the temperature. Cold food needs to be at 41 or below. Eggs and shellfish get a little more room — 45 is acceptable for them. If it is warmer than that send it back.

Deliveries must be rejected when TCS food arrives out of safe temperature range packaging is damaged or tampered with canned goods are dented swollen or leaking frozen food shows signs of thawing and refreezing or shellfish tags are missing.
KeyPoints: Temperature out of range|Damaged or tampered packaging|Dented

Content: Rejection questions — temperature violations damaged packaging dented swollen or leaking cans thawed frozen food missing shellfish tags. Any of these conditions requires rejection. The exam never rewards accepting questionable product.

You are the last line of defense before bad food gets into your kitchen. A dented can a warm delivery a bag with a hole in it — all of these get sent back. It is much easier to reject a delivery than to deal with a sick customer.
Shellfish tags must be kept on file for 90 days from the date the last shellfish from that container was sold or served. Tags provide traceability during foodborne illness investigations.
KeyPoints: Keep shellfish tags on file for 90 days|From date last shellfish from container was sold or served

Content: 90 days is the shellfish tag retention period. This specific number appears on the exam. Think of it as a 3-month commitment — the tag stays on file for 3 months after the last shellfish is served.

Every bag of oysters every container of clams comes with a tag. That tag tells investigators where the shellfish came from if someone gets sick. Keep those tags for 90 days — three months. File them somewhere you can find them.
The FIFO method — First In First Out — requires older stock to be rotated to the front and used before newer stock. This prevents food from expiring before it is used.
KeyPoints: First In First Out|Older stock rotated to front|Used before newer stock

Content: FIFO — First In First Out. Old stock in front new stock in back. The exam tests the concept not the acronym. The idea is always use the oldest product first.

When new stock arrives push the old stuff to the front. Use the old stuff first. This is called FIFO — First In First Out. It sounds simple but it prevents a lot of waste and a lot of expired food from being served.
Dry goods must be stored at least 6 inches off the floor in a cool dry area away from outside walls. This prevents moisture absorption pest access and allows for cleaning underneath.
KeyPoints: At least 6 inches off the floor|Cool

Content: Dry storage questions — 6 inches off the floor is the key number. Also away from outside walls and in a cool dry area. The 6-inch rule allows cleaning and prevents pests.

Never store food directly on the floor. Six inches off the ground — that is the rule. It keeps food away from pests that travel along the floor and lets you clean underneath. A simple shelf solves this completely.
Frozen food must be received completely solid with no signs of softness flexibility or large ice crystals. Large ice crystals indicate the food thawed and refroze during transport.
KeyPoints: Must be received completely solid|No softness or flexibility|Large ice crystals = thawed and refroze: reject

Content: Frozen food receiving questions — completely solid is the standard. Large ice crystals mean thaw-refreeze which means temperature abuse during transport. Reject it.

Frozen food should be rock solid when it arrives. If it is soft or flexible it thawed somewhere along the way. If it has big ice crystals inside it thawed and refroze. Both mean it was abused in transit. Send it back.

Cleaning removes dirt grease and organic matter from surfaces. Sanitizing reduces pathogens to safe levels. Both steps are required on food-contact surfaces — sanitizing alone on a dirty surface is ineffective.
KeyPoints: Cleaning removes dirt and organic matter|Sanitizing reduces pathogens to safe levels|Must clean before sanitizing|Sanitizer on dirty surface is ineffective

Content: Cleaning vs sanitizing is a fundamental exam distinction. Cleaning removes soil. Sanitizing kills pathogens. You must clean before you sanitize — sanitizer applied to a dirty surface cannot work properly.

Cleaning and sanitizing are two different things. Cleaning gets rid of the dirt you can see. Sanitizing kills the bacteria you cannot see. You have to do both — and always in that order. Clean first then sanitize.
The correct procedure for the three-compartment sink is scrape and pre-rinse then wash in detergent solution rinse in clean water sanitize in approved solution and air-dry completely.
KeyPoints: Scrape and pre-rinse → Wash in detergent → Rinse in clean water → Sanitize → Air-dry|Never towel-dry

Content: Three-compartment sink order — wash rinse sanitize air-dry. Any answer that skips a step reorders the steps or substitutes towel-drying for air-drying is wrong.

Three sinks three jobs. First sink — wash with hot soapy water. Second sink — rinse off the soap. Third sink — sanitize to kill bacteria. Then let everything air-dry. Never skip a step and never dry with a towel.
Chlorine sanitizer must be maintained between 50 and 99 ppm. Below 50 ppm it is ineffective. Above 99 ppm it becomes a chemical hazard and leaves toxic residue on food-contact surfaces.
KeyPoints: Effective range: 50–99 ppm|Below 50 ppm: ineffective|Above 99 ppm: chemical hazard|Test with test strips

Content: Chlorine range — 50 to 99 ppm. Too low and it does not work. Too high and it is dangerous. Test strips must be used to verify concentration. Always check strip expiration dates before testing.

Bleach sanitizer has a sweet spot — between 50 and 99 parts per million. Too weak and it does not kill bacteria. Too strong and it leaves chemicals on your equipment that can make people sick. Test it with test strips every time.
Sanitizer effectiveness is determined by concentration temperature pH and contact time. All four factors must be within the correct range for the sanitizer to work properly.
KeyPoints: Concentration|Temperature|pH|Contact time|All four must be within correct range

Content: Sanitizer effectiveness questions — concentration temperature pH contact time. If any of these is outside the recommended range sanitizing fails. Chlorine is most sensitive to pH and hardness.

Four things determine whether your sanitizer actually works — how strong it is how warm the water is the pH of the water and how long it stays in contact with the surface. Get all four right and it works. Miss one and it might not.
Food-contact surfaces must be cleaned and sanitized after each use after handling different raw proteins at minimum every 4 hours during continuous use and immediately after contamination.
KeyPoints: After each use|After handling different raw proteins|Every 4 hours during continuous use|Immediately after contamination

Content: Cleaning frequency questions — after each use after raw protein contact every 4 hours during continuous use and immediately after contamination. The 4-hour rule applies to surfaces in constant use.

Your prep table your cutting board your slicer — clean and sanitize them every time you switch to a different food and at least every 4 hours if you are using them continuously. After raw chicken — always immediately.
A high-temperature dish machine must reach 180°F on dish surfaces during the final rinse. A low-temperature machine uses chemical sanitizer and requires a minimum rinse temperature of 120°F.
KeyPoints: High-temp machine: 180°F on dish surface during final rinse|Low-temp machine: chemical sanitizer

Content: Dish machine temperature questions — high-temp final rinse is 180°F on the dish surface. Low-temp uses chemicals and requires 120°F. These two numbers appear frequently on the exam.

Two kinds of dish machines. The hot one kills bacteria with heat — it has to hit 180 on the surface of the dishes. The chemical one uses sanitizer instead of extreme heat — water just needs to be 120. Know which kind you have.

The four approved thawing methods are in a refrigerator at 41°F or below under running water at 70°F or below in a microwave if cooked immediately and as part of the cooking process. Room temperature thawing is never acceptable.
KeyPoints: Refrigerator at 41°F or below|Running water at 70°F or below|Microwave — cook immediately after|Cooking from frozen|Counter thawing: never acceptable

Content: Thawing method questions — refrigerator running water microwave-then-cook cook from frozen. Counter thawing is always wrong. Running water must be 70°F or below and continuous not standing.

Four ways to thaw food safely. In the fridge overnight. Under cold running water. In the microwave if you cook it right after. Or just cook it from frozen. That is it. The counter is never on the list.
All produce must be washed under cool running water before cutting peeling or cooking. Washing after cutting can spread surface pathogens into the interior of the produce.
KeyPoints: Wash under cool running water before cutting

Content: Produce washing questions — wash before cutting not after. Cutting unwashed produce drags surface contaminants into the interior. Running water is the required method.
Always wash your fruits and vegetables before you cut them. Once you cut into them any bacteria on the surface gets dragged inside where it can grow. Wash first cut second.

Food contaminated by physical hazards such as glass metal or bone or by chemical hazards such as cleaners or pesticides must be discarded immediately. These hazards cannot be corrected by cooking or rinsing.
KeyPoints: Physical hazards: glass

Content: Contamination questions — glass metal chemicals in food always means discard. The exam will offer rinsing or cooking as solutions. Neither works for physical or chemical contamination.
If a chemical gets into food or you find glass or metal in it — throw it all out. You cannot cook out a chemical. You cannot rinse out broken glass. The only safe answer is discard and start over.
Internal temperatures must be verified using a calibrated food thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the food. Color texture and cooking time are not reliable indicators of doneness.
KeyPoints: Use calibrated thermometer|Insert into thickest part|Color and time are NOT reliable indicators

Content: Thermometer questions — calibrated thermometer inserted into the thickest part. Color and time are never reliable. The only way to verify safe cooking is temperature measurement.
You cannot tell if food is cooked safely by looking at it. Chicken can look done and still be 140 degrees inside. Use a thermometer every time. Put it in the thickest part and wait for the number.
Partial cooking must not exceed 60 minutes in the initial cook. The food must be cooled immediately after partial cooking and finished cooking to the required temperature before service.
KeyPoints: Initial cook: 60 minutes maximum|Cool immediately after|Finish to required temperature before service

Content: Partial cooking questions — 60 minutes maximum initial cook cool immediately and finish to required temperature before service. The 60-minute limit is the number the exam tests.

If you partially cook something — like par-cooking chicken for later — you get 60 minutes maximum on that first cook. Then cool it immediately. When you finish cooking it later it still has to hit the full required temperature.
Ice used for beverages or food contact must be handled with a scoop and stored in a clean covered container. Ice is a food and must be protected from contamination like any other food.
KeyPoints: Handle with scoop only|Store in clean covered container|Never use bare hands or a glass as scoop|Ice is a food

Content: Ice handling questions — always a scoop never bare hands never a glass used as a scoop. Ice is a food. Contaminating the ice supply contaminates everything it touches.

Ice is food. Treat it like food. Use a scoop to handle it — not your hands not a cup. And never let the scoop sit inside the ice machine where it can pick up bacteria. Hang it on the side or store it separately.

Handwashing sinks must be used exclusively for handwashing be stocked with soap single-use towels and a waste bin provide running water at a minimum of 100°F and have a handwashing reminder sign posted.
KeyPoints: Exclusive use for handwashing only|Stocked with soap

Content: Handwashing sink questions — exclusive use soap towels waste bin 100°F water and a posted sign. Any other use of the handwashing sink is a violation. A blocked or unstocked sink is also a violation.

The handwashing sink has one job. Handwashing. Not rinsing equipment not dumping ice not storing supplies. One job. Keep it stocked with soap and paper towels and make sure nothing is blocking it ever.

Adequate lighting must be provided in all food prep areas storage areas and warewashing areas so that staff can safely handle food read labels and identify contamination. Light fixtures must have shatter-resistant covers.
KeyPoints: Adequate lighting in all food prep

Content: Lighting questions — adequate for safe food handling and shatter-resistant covers over fixtures. The cover requirement prevents glass contamination if a bulb breaks during food preparation.

If you cannot see clearly in your walk-in or your prep area that is a food safety problem. You need to be able to read labels check dates and see what you are working with. And every light fixture needs a cover so broken glass does not fall into food.

A backflow prevention device prevents contaminated water from reversing flow into the potable water supply. An air gap — physical separation between a water outlet and the flood level rim of a sink — is the most reliable method.
KeyPoints: Air gap is most reliable method|Physical separation between water outlet and flood rim|Prevents contaminated water from reversing into potable supply

Content: Backflow prevention questions — air gap is the most reliable method. It creates physical separation that cannot fail mechanically. Cross-connections between potable and non-potable water are a critical violation.

Dirty water flowing backwards into your clean water supply is called backflow. The best way to prevent it is an air gap — a physical space between where the water comes out and where it could get contaminated. No mechanical device needed.
Floor-mounted equipment must be elevated at least 6 inches off the floor or sealed directly to the floor with no gaps. This allows cleaning underneath and eliminates pest harborage spaces.
KeyPoints: Elevated at least 6 inches off floor OR sealed directly to floor|Allows cleaning underneath|Eliminates pest harborage

Content: Floor-mounted equipment questions — 6 inches off the floor or sealed to the floor. The purpose is cleaning access and pest prevention. This number appears on the exam.
Big equipment like fryers and prep tables needs to be up off the floor on legs — at least 6 inches. That space lets you clean underneath. If you cannot clean under it pests will live under it.
Garbage containers in food prep areas must have tight-fitting lids be cleaned regularly on a schedule and be kept away from food prep surfaces. Lids prevent pest access and reduce odors.
KeyPoints: Tight-fitting lids required|Cleaned regularly|Kept away from food prep surfaces

Content: Garbage container questions — tight-fitting lids regular cleaning and proper placement away from food surfaces. Lids are the key feature that prevents pest access to food waste.

Garbage cans without lids are an open invitation to pests. A fly lands on the garbage then lands on your food. Keep lids on all garbage cans in the kitchen and clean the cans regularly so they do not build up residue that attracts pests.

Integrated pest management begins with eliminating the three things pests need — food water and shelter. Entry points are sealed harborage areas are eliminated and food is stored properly. Chemical treatments are a last resort.
KeyPoints: Deny food

Content: IPM questions — deny food water shelter. Seal entry points eliminate harborage. Chemical treatment is done by a licensed pest control operator only. Prevention is always the preferred approach.

Pests come into your facility because it offers them three things — food water and a place to hide. Take those three things away and they have no reason to stay. Seal the gaps clean the drains store the food properly.

Common pest entry points include gaps under doors cracks in walls openings around pipes and utility lines torn window screens and propped-open doors. All openings must be sealed with approved materials.
KeyPoints: Gaps under doors|Cracks in walls|Openings around pipes|Torn window screens|Propped-open doors|All must be sealed

Content: Pest entry point questions — gaps under doors pipe penetrations torn screens propped doors. Even a dime-sized gap can admit a mouse. All openings must be sealed. Self-closing doors and door sweeps work together.

Mice can squeeze through a hole the size of a dime. Cockroaches can get through a crack you can barely see. Walk the perimeter of your facility and seal every gap every crack every hole around every pipe. Every one.

Common signs of pest infestation include droppings nesting materials gnaw marks on packaging greasy rub marks along walls webbing in corners and unusual odors. Early identification enables faster response.
KeyPoints: Droppings|Nesting materials|Gnaw marks on packaging|Greasy rub marks along walls|Unusual odors

Content: Pest identification questions — droppings gnaw marks greasy rub marks along walls are rodent signs. Musty oily odor and dark specks are cockroach signs. Early signs require immediate action not documentation only.

Learn what pest evidence looks like before you have a problem. Rodent droppings look like dark grains of rice. Cockroaches leave dark smears and a musty oily smell. Gnaw marks on bags mean something is eating your food.

When pests are found a licensed pest control operator must be contacted immediately. Food service staff must never apply pesticides themselves. All food in affected areas must be inspected and contaminated product discarded.
KeyPoints: Contact licensed pest control operator immediately|Staff never apply pesticides|Inspect and discard contaminated food in affected areas

Content: Pest response questions — licensed pest control operator only. Staff never apply pesticides. Inspect all food in affected areas. The exam will offer setting traps yourself as an option. It is not correct.

If you find evidence of pests do not grab a can of Raid and start spraying. You need a licensed professional. Pesticides used incorrectly in a food facility can contaminate food and make people sick. Call the pest control company.
Cardboard boxes provide ideal harborage for cockroaches and must be removed from the facility as soon as product is unpacked. Clutter dark corners and equipment gaps also create harborage areas.
KeyPoints: Cardboard = primary cockroach harborage|Remove immediately after unpacking|Eliminate clutter and dark spaces

Content: Harborage questions — cardboard is a primary cockroach harborage. Remove it immediately after unpacking. Clutter and dark spaces also create harborage. Eliminating harborage is a core IPM principle.
Cockroaches love cardboard boxes. They hide in the corrugated layers and hitchhike right into your facility. As soon as you unpack a delivery break down the boxes and get them out of the building.

Active managerial control means the person in charge proactively identifies and corrects food safety risks before they cause illness. It requires ongoing observation correction training and documentation.
KeyPoints: Proactively identify and correct food safety risks|Ongoing observation

Content: Active managerial control questions — proactive not reactive. The manager observes corrects trains and documents. Any answer suggesting a manager waits for a problem or documents without correcting is wrong.

Being in charge of food safety does not mean waiting for something to go wrong. It means walking the floor watching what your team does correcting them in the moment and making sure they understand why. Every shift.

The seven HACCP principles are hazard analysis identification of critical control points establishment of critical limits monitoring corrective action verification and record keeping. HACCP is a proactive system for controlling food safety hazards.
KeyPoints: Seven principles: Hazard analysis → CCPs → Critical limits → Monitoring → Corrective action → Verification → Record keeping

Content: HACCP questions test the seven principles and key definitions. A CCP is a point of control. A critical limit is the measurable boundary. Monitoring is ongoing observation. Corrective action is the response when a limit is not met.

HACCP sounds complicated but it is just a system for making sure the most dangerous steps in your process are under control. You identify where things can go wrong you set limits you watch for problems and you fix them fast.
A critical control point is a step in the food handling process where a hazard can be prevented eliminated or reduced to a safe level. Cooking cooling and receiving are common CCPs.
KeyPoints: A CCP is a step where a hazard can be prevented

Content: CCP questions — a CCP is where you can control a hazard not just where a hazard exists. Cooking is a CCP because heat destroys pathogens. Receiving is a CCP because you can reject unsafe food.

A critical control point is your chance to stop a problem before it becomes a disaster. Cooking is a CCP — if you hit the right temperature the bacteria die. Receiving is a CCP — if you reject bad product it never enters your kitchen.

When a critical limit is not met at a CCP corrective action must be taken immediately. The corrective action addresses the specific failure brings the process back into control and ensures unsafe food is not served.
KeyPoints: Take corrective action immediately when critical limit is not met|Address the specific failure|Unsafe food must not be served

Content: Corrective action questions — immediate response to a CCP failure. The action must address the specific problem. Documenting without correcting is never acceptable. Discarding food that failed a CCP is a valid corrective action.

When something goes wrong at a critical point you fix it right now. You do not write it down and keep going. If the temperature was not met you reheat or discard. If the delivery was out of range you send it back. Fix first document second.

Managers are responsible for ensuring all food handlers receive food safety training before handling food understand the operation's food safety policies and are retrained when violations are observed.
KeyPoints: Train before handling food|Ongoing — not just at hire|Retrain when violations are observed

Content: Manager training questions — before handling food not after. Ongoing not just at hire. Retrain when violations are observed not at the next performance review. Active not passive.
Your team cannot follow rules they do not know. Train every new hire before they touch food. When you see someone do something wrong correct them right then and show them the right way. Do not wait.

A strong food safety culture starts with management consistently modeling and enforcing safe practices. When managers take food safety seriously staff follow. Culture is built through daily habits not annual training.
KeyPoints: Management must consistently model and enforce safe practices|Culture built through daily habits|Not annual training

Content: Food safety culture questions — management sets the tone. Consistent enforcement matters more than periodic training. The exam favors answers where the manager actively observes corrects and reinforces.

Your team watches what you do more than they listen to what you say. If you take shortcuts they will take shortcuts. If you wash your hands every time they will wash their hands every time. You set the standard every single day.
